run.output: Query returned 1 rows, Execution time: 32 ms

validation.comparison_result: Q6 results match ground truth: revenue=1230113636.0101

profiling.notes: perf tool not available in environment

Q6.notes: Single aggregate result, 60M-row lineitem scan with 4 independent filters

Q6.bottleneck: Scalar predicate evaluation and I/O prefetching for HDD access pattern
Q6.root_cause: Query scans 60M rows (59986052) with 4 independent predicates. Current implementation uses scalar comparisons (no SIMD) and does not optimize predicate ordering by selectivity. With 64 cores and HDD storage, morsel-driven parallelism is effective, but per-row predicate cost is not minimized.

analysis.overall: Q6 is fundamentally sound: it uses thread parallelism with morsel-driven execution across 64 cores, thread-local aggregation, and zero-copy mmap access. Results are correct. Performance is 32 ms vs DuckDB baseline of 19 ms (1.68x gap). GenDB baseline is 29.3 ms, so room for 1-2 ms improvement exists. The remaining gap likely comes from: (1) scalar vs SIMD predicate evaluation (4 filters × 60M rows = 240M comparisons), (2) suboptimal predicate ordering (discount is most selective at 9% but evaluated after shipdate at 14.5%), (3) lack of madvise hints for HDD sequential I/O. No correctness issues or crashes. The implementation is well-optimized for current hardware; further gains require micro-level optimizations (SIMD, predicate reordering, I/O hints).

performance_optimizations.description: Reorder predicate evaluation from (shipdate, discount, quantity) to (discount, shipdate, quantity) based on selectivity values from workload analysis. Discount BETWEEN has selectivity 0.09 (9% of rows pass), shipdate range has 0.145 (14.5%), and quantity has 0.48 (48%). Evaluating most selective first (discount) reduces rows passed to later predicates by ~11x before shipdate, then another 6.9x before quantity. This avoids ~51.4M scalar comparisons on discount and quantity for non-matching rows.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 1-2 ms (6-12% reduction). Estimation: ~90M unnecessary predicate evaluations eliminated (discount+quantity checks on rows filtered by shipdate). At ~1-2 CPU cycles per comparison, saves 90-180M cycles ≈ 45-90 ns per row × 60M rows ÷ effective_pipeline_depth.

performance_optimizations.description: Replace scalar predicate evaluations with SIMD instructions. For the discount range check, use _mm256_cmpge_pd and _mm256_cmple_pd to compare 4 double values per instruction. Similarly for quantity < 24.0 use _mm256_cmplt_pd. Combine results with _mm256_and_pd. For int32_t shipdate, use _mm256_cmpgt_epi32 and _mm256_cmplt_epi32 (8 ints per instruction). Structure loop to process 4-8 rows at a time, accumulate partial sums in SIMD registers (_mm256_add_pd for doubles), then horizontal reduction to scalar at loop end. Align column arrays to 32-byte boundaries to enable aligned loads (_mm256_load_pd).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-3 ms (6-10% reduction). SIMD can process 4-8 values per instruction vs 1 for scalar. Current: 240M scalar comparisons (60M rows × 4 predicates). With SIMD: 60M rows ÷ 4 = 15M vector instructions for compound predicate. Assuming 4 cycles per vector instruction and 0.25 cycles per scalar, saves ~180M cycles ≈ 90 ms wall-clock ÷ 30 cores active ≈ 3 ms improvement. Actual gain depends on compiler vectorization and memory bandwidth.

performance_optimizations.technique: madvise Hints for HDD Sequential Access
performance_optimizations.description: After mmapping column files (shipdate, discount, quantity, extendedprice), call madvise(ptr, size, MADV_SEQUENTIAL) on each mmap region. This tells the OS kernel to optimize page-in order for sequential scans on HDD, reducing disk head seeks. Also consider MADV_WILLNEED to prefetch all column pages into page cache before scanning if memory budget allows (376 GB available, columns are ~1.9 GB total for 60M rows × 4 doubles ≈ 1.9 GB).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 0.5-1.5 ms (1-5% reduction). HDD random access = ~5-10 ms per seek, sequential = amortized through OS buffering. With MADV_SEQUENTIAL, kernel optimizes readahead window. Expected gain depends on page cache state and HDD characteristics. Conservative estimate: 10-20% I/O improvement on column fetch phase (if I/O bound) = 0.5-1 ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Zone Map Skipping for Shipdate Range Predicate
performance_optimizations.description: Lineitem table has l_shipdate_sorted index (from storage_design.json). Use zone map metadata (per-block min/max) to skip blocks that fall entirely outside the predicate range [1994-01-01, 1994-12-31]. Each 100K-row block has recorded min/max shipdate; if block_max < 8766 or block_min > 9130, skip the entire block. This avoids ~5.4M useless row checks (9% × 60M rows). Implementation: Load zone map metadata from storage, iterate blocks, check predicate range overlap, collect qualifying block offsets, mmap only those regions.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 0.5-1.5 ms (1-5% reduction). Skips ~91% × 60M × 100K blocks = ~5.4M rows of unnecessary work (predicate evaluation + data access). At 1-2 cycles per row, saves 5.4-10.8M cycles ≈ 3-5 ms theoretical max. Practical: limited by block granularity (100K rows) and I/O overlap with computation; expect 0.5-1.5 ms.

summary: Q6 iteration 0 passes all correctness checks with precise match (1230113636.0101) to ground truth. Current execution time of 32 ms is 1.68x slower than DuckDB baseline (19 ms) but only 1.09x slower than GenDB baseline (29.3 ms). The implementation is well-architected: uses 64-core parallelism with morsel-driven execution, thread-local aggregation, and zero-copy mmap. Performance bottleneck is in scalar predicate evaluation (240M comparisons across 4 filters on 60M rows). No critical fixes needed. Recommended optimizations (in priority order): (1) Predicate reordering by selectivity (discount first at 9%, then shipdate at 14.5%, then quantity at 48%) to reduce unnecessary comparisons — expect 1-2 ms gain; (2) SIMD vectorization of all 4 predicates using AVX2/AVX-512 to process 4-8 rows per instruction — expect 2-3 ms gain; (3) madvise hints for HDD sequential access — expect 0.5-1.5 ms gain; (4) Zone map block skipping on shipdate to avoid 5.4M unnecessary rows — expect 0.5-1.5 ms gain. Combined potential: 4-8 ms improvement to reach ~24-28 ms, approaching DuckDB's 19 ms. Next iteration should focus on predicate reordering (low risk, quick win) and SIMD (medium risk, higher gain).
